quiz_questions.question_text: Which class of proteins drives the cell cycle forward by binding to and activating cyclin-dependent kinases (Cdks)?

options.text: Phosphatases
Histones
Cyclins
Tumor suppressors

quiz_questions.explanation: Cyclins are regulatory proteins whose concentrations fluctuate during the cell cycle. When cyclins bind to Cdks, the resulting complexes phosphorylate target proteins that advance the cell through specific phases of the cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: The G1 checkpoint primarily assesses whether the cell:

options.text: Has completed DNA replication without errors
Has sufficient size, nutrients, and undamaged DNA to proceed to S phase
Has properly aligned chromosomes at the metaphase plate
Has separated all sister chromatids

quiz_questions.explanation: The G1 checkpoint (also called the restriction point in animal cells) evaluates cell size, nutrient availability, growth factor signals, and DNA integrity before committing to DNA replication in S phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which tumor suppressor protein is known as the 'guardian of the genome' because it halts the cell cycle when DNA damage is detected?

options.text: Cyclin D
Ras
Myc

quiz_questions.explanation: p53 is a transcription factor that responds to DNA damage by activating genes that halt the cell cycle (e.g., p21), giving the cell time to repair DNA or, if damage is irreparable, triggering apoptosis. Mutations in p53 are found in over 50% of human cancers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What role do growth factors play in cell cycle regulation?

options.text: They form the spindle apparatus
They directly replicate DNA
They act as external signals that stimulate cells to enter the cell cycle and divide
They destroy damaged cells through apoptosis

quiz_questions.explanation: Growth factors are extracellular signaling proteins that bind to receptors on target cells, activating intracellular signaling cascades that promote entry into the cell cycle and cell division.

quiz_questions.question_text: A proto-oncogene becomes an oncogene when it:

options.text: Is converted into a tumor suppressor gene
Moves from the nucleus to the cytoplasm
Is deleted from the genome entirely
Undergoes a mutation that causes it to be overactive, promoting excessive cell division

quiz_questions.explanation: Proto-oncogenes are normal genes that code for proteins promoting cell growth. When mutated (e.g., point mutation, gene amplification, or translocation), they become oncogenes that are constitutively active, driving uncontrolled cell proliferation.

quiz_questions.question_text: The spindle assembly checkpoint (M checkpoint) ensures that:

options.text: DNA has been fully replicated before mitosis begins
Cyclins have been completely degraded after S phase
Growth factors are present in the extracellular environment
All chromosomes are properly attached to spindle fibers before anaphase proceeds

quiz_questions.explanation: The spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) at the metaphase-to-anaphase transition monitors whether all kinetochores are properly attached to spindle microtubules. If any chromosome is unattached, anaphase is delayed to prevent unequal chromosome distribution.

quiz_questions.question_text: Retinoblastoma protein (Rb) prevents cell cycle progression by:

options.text: Binding to and inhibiting E2F transcription factors that promote entry into S phase
Blocking growth factor receptors on the cell surface
Directly degrading cyclins
Phosphorylating Cdks to make them inactive

quiz_questions.explanation: Rb is a tumor suppressor that, in its unphosphorylated form, binds and inhibits E2F transcription factors needed for S phase gene expression. When cyclin-Cdk complexes phosphorylate Rb, E2F is released and the cell proceeds to S phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cells that exit the cell cycle and enter a non-dividing state are said to be in:

options.text: G0 phase
G2 phase
M phase
S phase

quiz_questions.explanation: G0 is a quiescent state in which cells are metabolically active but no longer dividing. Some cells (like neurons and muscle cells) remain in G0 permanently, while others can re-enter the cell cycle if stimulated.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following correctly describes density-dependent inhibition of cell growth?

options.text: Cells divide faster when they are crowded together
Cells stop dividing when they contact neighboring cells and the available surface is covered
Cells increase cyclin production when growth factors are absent
Cells require direct sunlight to divide

quiz_questions.explanation: Density-dependent inhibition (contact inhibition) causes normal cells to stop dividing once they form a single layer covering the available surface. Cell-cell contacts trigger signaling pathways that halt the cell cycle, a property often lost in cancer cells.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cancer typically requires mutations in multiple genes. Why is a single mutation usually insufficient to cause cancer?

options.text: Single mutations always result in apoptosis
Only mutations in sex chromosomes can cause cancer
Cells have multiple redundant regulatory mechanisms (checkpoints, tumor suppressors, DNA repair) that must all be compromised
A single mutation will always be repaired by the immune system

quiz_questions.explanation: Cells possess multiple layers of defense against uncontrolled division, including growth factor requirements, checkpoint controls, DNA repair enzymes, tumor suppressors, and apoptotic pathways. Cancer usually develops only after several of these safeguards are disabled by accumulated mutations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'cell cycle checkpoint'?

options.text: A physical barrier in the cell that prevents organelles from moving
A critical control point in the cell cycle where the cell evaluates internal and external conditions before proceeding to the next phase
The point at which the cell membrane divides during cytokinesis
A region of the chromosome where spindle fibers attach

quiz_questions.explanation: Cell cycle checkpoints are regulatory mechanisms at specific points (G1, G2, and M) where the cell assesses conditions like DNA integrity, cell size, and proper chromosome attachment before committing to the next phase of division.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'cyclin-dependent kinase (Cdk)' mean?

options.text: A cyclin protein that depends on kinase activity to be synthesized
A membrane receptor that detects growth factors
A kinase that functions independently without any binding partners
A protein kinase that is only active when bound to a cyclin protein, and drives the cell cycle by phosphorylating key target proteins

quiz_questions.explanation: Cyclin-dependent kinases (Cdks) are enzymes that phosphorylate target proteins to advance the cell cycle, but they are only active when bound to their cyclin partners. Different cyclin-Cdk complexes regulate different phases of the cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'tumor suppressor gene'?

options.text: A gene whose normal function is to inhibit cell division, promote DNA repair, or trigger apoptosis, thereby preventing tumor formation
A gene that, when mutated, directly promotes cell division when considering all relevant suppressor factors
A gene found only in cancerous cells, especially during periods of significant change
A gene that encodes growth factors, especially during periods of significant change

quiz_questions.explanation: Tumor suppressor genes encode proteins that normally restrain cell division (e.g., p53, Rb). When both copies of a tumor suppressor gene are lost or inactivated by mutation, the cell loses a critical brake on proliferation, increasing cancer risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'proto-oncogene'?

options.text: A gene that suppresses tumor formation
A viral gene inserted into the host genome
A mutated gene that causes uncontrolled cell growth
A normal cellular gene that codes for proteins involved in cell growth and division, which can become an oncogene if mutated

quiz_questions.explanation: Proto-oncogenes are normal genes encoding proteins that promote cell growth, division, and survival (e.g., growth factors, signal transduction proteins, transcription factors). Mutations converting them to oncogenes result in overactive proteins that drive uncontrolled proliferation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'contact inhibition' mean?

options.text: The inhibition of enzyme activity when enzymes are in contact with inhibitors
A mechanism by which immune cells directly kill tumor cells upon contact
A property of normal cells in which cell-to-cell contact signals the cells to stop dividing
A process that prevents cell fusion during development

quiz_questions.explanation: Contact inhibition is a growth-regulatory mechanism in which normal cells stop dividing once they touch neighboring cells and form a confluent monolayer. Cancer cells typically lose contact inhibition and continue to pile up, forming tumors.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'apoptosis' in the context of cell cycle regulation?

options.text: The process by which cells become cancerous according to widely held views on this topic
Programmed cell death triggered by signaling pathways to eliminate cells with irreparable damage or that are no longer needed
The uncontrolled division of cells when considering all relevant apoptosis factors
The arrest of the cell cycle at G0 phase, at least according to standard theory

quiz_questions.explanation: Apoptosis is a regulated form of cell death that eliminates damaged, infected, or unnecessary cells. In cell cycle regulation, apoptosis acts as a fail-safe: if DNA damage cannot be repaired, p53 and other proteins trigger apoptosis to prevent the propagation of mutations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'metastasis' mean?

options.text: The initial mutation that transforms a normal cell into a cancer cell
The natural process of cell death that removes old cells
The growth of a benign tumor that remains in one location
The spread of cancer cells from the original tumor to distant sites in the body through the blood or lymphatic system

quiz_questions.explanation: Metastasis is the process by which cancer cells break away from the primary tumor, enter the bloodstream or lymphatic system, and establish secondary tumors at distant sites. Metastasis is what makes cancer particularly dangerous and difficult to treat.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'growth factor'?

options.text: A hormone produced only during embryonic development
A nutrient molecule such as glucose required for cell metabolism
An internal checkpoint protein that halts the cell cycle
An extracellular signaling protein that binds to specific receptors on target cells and stimulates cell growth and division

quiz_questions.explanation: Growth factors are proteins secreted by cells that stimulate proliferation of target cells by binding to cell-surface receptors and activating intracellular signaling pathways (e.g., PDGF, EGF). They are important external regulators of the cell cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'anchorage dependence' mean in cell biology?

options.text: The attachment of spindle fibers to kinetochores during mitosis
The requirement that cells must be attached to a substratum (such as the extracellular matrix) in order to divide
The tendency of cells to anchor to each other through tight junctions
The process by which cells attach to blood vessel walls during metastasis

quiz_questions.explanation: Anchorage dependence is a property of normal cells that requires them to be attached to a surface (extracellular matrix or other substratum) to grow and divide. Cancer cells often lose anchorage dependence, allowing them to grow in suspension and metastasize.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'G0 phase'?

options.text: The phase during which DNA is replicated
The initial phase of apoptosis
The gap between meiosis I and meiosis II
A resting or quiescent state in which cells have exited the active cell cycle and are not preparing to divide

quiz_questions.explanation: G0 is a quiescent phase where cells exit the cell cycle and stop dividing. Some cells (like neurons) remain in G0 permanently, while others (like liver cells) can re-enter the cycle in response to appropriate stimuli.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient is diagnosed with Li-Fraumeni syndrome, a hereditary condition caused by a germline mutation in one copy of the TP53 gene. The patient's cells still have one functional copy of TP53. Why does this patient have a significantly higher cancer risk than the general population?

options.text: Only one additional mutation (loss of the remaining functional TP53 allele) is needed to completely eliminate p53 tumor suppressor function in any cell
One mutant copy of TP53 actively promotes cell division
The mutant p53 protein acts as a growth factor
Having one mutant TP53 allele prevents all DNA repair in the cell

quiz_questions.explanation: This illustrates the two-hit hypothesis for tumor suppressor genes. The patient inherits one defective TP53 allele, so only one somatic mutation (the 'second hit') in any cell is needed to eliminate p53 function entirely, greatly increasing cancer susceptibility.

quiz_questions.question_text: A research team develops a drug that prevents the degradation of cyclins by the proteasome. What effect would this drug have on cells?

options.text: Cells might continue cycling without proper regulation because cyclin-Cdk complexes would remain persistently active
Cells would undergo apoptosis immediately
Cells would be arrested in G0 because no cyclins would be available
Cells would be unable to enter S phase because Cdks would remain inactive

quiz_questions.explanation: Normal cell cycle progression depends on the timely degradation of cyclins by the proteasome. If cyclins are not degraded, Cdks remain active, and the cell may continue dividing without proper checkpoint regulation, similar to what occurs in some cancers.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist observes that cancer cells from a biopsy continue to proliferate in a culture dish even without added growth factors, while normal cells in the same dish stop dividing. Which alteration most likely explains the cancer cells' behavior?

options.text: The cancer cells have stopped performing cellular respiration
The cancer cells have a mutation that causes them to produce their own growth signals or have a constitutively active signaling pathway
The cancer cells are absorbing growth factors from the normal cells
The cancer cells have more mitochondria than normal cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Cancer cells often acquire mutations that make them independent of external growth factors, such as producing their own growth signals (autocrine stimulation) or having constitutively active receptors or downstream signaling proteins (e.g., mutant Ras).

quiz_questions.question_text: Chemotherapy drugs like taxol stabilize microtubules, preventing their disassembly. At which checkpoint would this drug most effectively arrest cancer cells?

options.text: G2 checkpoint
Spindle assembly checkpoint (M checkpoint)
G1 checkpoint
S phase checkpoint

quiz_questions.explanation: Taxol prevents microtubule depolymerization, so spindle fibers cannot properly attach to and detach from kinetochores. The spindle assembly checkpoint detects improper attachments and arrests cells in metaphase, preventing completion of mitosis.

quiz_questions.question_text: HeLa cells, derived from cervical cancer, have been growing in laboratory culture since 1951 and show no signs of slowing proliferation. Normal human cells undergo senescence after about 50 divisions (the Hayflick limit). What properties of HeLa cells most likely allow them to bypass this limit?

options.text: HeLa cells perform meiosis instead of mitosis, resetting the division counter each time
HeLa cells lack a nucleus and therefore cannot track the number of divisions
HeLa cells obtain energy from light, reducing the wear on their DNA
HeLa cells have activated telomerase and disrupted tumor suppressor pathways, allowing unlimited division

quiz_questions.explanation: HeLa cells express active telomerase, which rebuilds telomeres after each division, preventing the progressive shortening that normally leads to senescence. Combined with mutations in p53 and Rb pathways, these cells are effectively immortal.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician prescribes imatinib (Gleevec) to a patient with chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML). Imatinib specifically inhibits the BCR-ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase produced by a chromosomal translocation. How does this drug combat cancer?

options.text: It activates p53 in all cells of the body
It blocks the overactive kinase signaling that drives uncontrolled cell division in CML cells specifically
It prevents DNA replication in all rapidly dividing cells
It destroys the Philadelphia chromosome in every cell

quiz_questions.explanation: Imatinib is a targeted therapy that selectively binds to the ATP-binding site of the BCR-ABL kinase, blocking its activity. This halts the constitutive growth signaling in CML cells while largely sparing normal cells, exemplifying precision medicine.

quiz_questions.question_text: Researchers studying wound healing observe that skin cells near a wound begin dividing rapidly, but once the wound closes and cells make contact with each other, division stops. A nearby skin cancer, however, continues to grow past the wound boundaries. Which two regulatory properties have the cancer cells lost?

options.text: The ability to undergo S phase and G2 phase
Contact inhibition and density-dependent inhibition of growth
DNA replication and transcription
Cyclin production and Cdk activation

quiz_questions.explanation: Normal cells stop dividing when they contact neighboring cells (contact inhibition) and when they reach a certain density (density-dependent inhibition). Cancer cells lose both of these properties, continuing to divide and pile up into disorganized masses.

quiz_questions.question_text: A molecular biologist finds that a cancer cell line has a mutation that causes Rb protein to be permanently phosphorylated. What is the predicted effect on cell cycle regulation?

options.text: The cell cycle would be permanently halted because phosphorylated Rb blocks division
Cyclins would be unable to bind to Cdks
E2F transcription factors would be permanently released, continuously promoting S phase entry and cell division
p53 would be activated and cause immediate apoptosis

quiz_questions.explanation: Rb normally inhibits E2F when unphosphorylated. If Rb is permanently phosphorylated, it can never bind and inhibit E2F, so E2F continuously activates genes required for DNA replication and S phase entry, promoting uncontrolled cell proliferation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A genetic test reveals that a patient carries a BRCA1 mutation, which impairs the cell's ability to repair double-strand DNA breaks. The patient's doctor recommends increased cancer screening. Why does impaired DNA repair increase cancer risk even though BRCA1 is not a traditional cell cycle regulator?

options.text: BRCA1 produces cyclins that are needed for normal cell cycle progression
BRCA1 mutations directly activate oncogenes
Without effective DNA repair, mutations in proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes accumulate more rapidly, accelerating the path to cancer
BRCA1 mutations prevent all cell division, causing compensatory growth in other tissues

quiz_questions.explanation: BRCA1 is a caretaker gene involved in homologous recombination repair of DNA double-strand breaks. When it is defective, DNA damage accumulates at a higher rate, increasing the likelihood that critical cell cycle genes (oncogenes, tumor suppressors) will acquire cancer-driving mutations.

quiz_questions.question_text: A clinical trial tests a new cancer drug that restores p53 function in tumor cells carrying p53 mutations. If the drug works as intended, what cellular responses would you expect to observe in the treated tumor cells?

options.text: Cell cycle arrest at the G1 checkpoint and/or induction of apoptosis in cells with damaged DNA
Loss of all checkpoint function
Increased cell division and tumor growth
Activation of oncogenes that promote metastasis

quiz_questions.explanation: Restoring p53 function would reactivate the G1 checkpoint, halting division of cells with DNA damage. Cells with irreparable damage would undergo p53-mediated apoptosis. This dual response would slow tumor growth and eliminate the most severely damaged cancer cells.
